content.text: Consent Capture for Claude-Powered Features

When an enterprise deploys Claude as a capability inside a product or workflow, any personal data that flows through a prompt or response requires a documented legal basis under privacy regulations such as GDPR, CCPA, and sector-specific laws. Consent capture is the mechanism by which the organization collects, stores, and associates a user's agreement with specific AI-powered features before invoking Claude on their behalf.

attrs.alt: Consent capture flow diagram for Claude-powered features

content.text: The flow above shows the two paths every Claude feature invocation must traverse: check for a stored consent record before making the API call, and if no record exists, present the consent dialog before proceeding. A declined consent causes the feature to be blocked and the refusal to be logged.
Why Consent Capture Is Not Optional

Claude processes text that often contains personal data: names, job roles, project details, customer information. Even when users supply that data themselves, processing it through a third-party AI model constitutes a separate processing activity that must be disclosed and justified. The enterprise cannot rely on the original collection consent for this new purpose.
GDPR requires a specific, informed, and freely given consent or an alternative lawful basis for each processing activity — including AI inference.
CCPA requires businesses to disclose when personal information is used with third-party AI providers and to honor opt-outs.
Financial and healthcare regulations (SOX, HIPAA) impose additional restrictions on sharing data with AI vendors even under contract.
Internal AI governance policies at regulated enterprises typically require documented user acknowledgement before data enters any AI pipeline.

The consent record is also your audit trail. If a regulatory body asks 'who authorized the use of employee X's data in your AI system and when?', the answer must come from a timestamped, tamper-evident consent log — not from a generic terms-of-service acceptance buried in an onboarding flow.
Consent Types and Legal Bases
Not every Claude feature requires the same consent mechanism. The appropriate approach depends on the data processed, the user relationship, and the regulatory jurisdiction:
Consent Type
Legal Basis
Capture Mechanism
Scope
Explicit opt-in
GDPR Art. 6(1)(a)
Modal dialog, unchecked checkbox
Feature-specific
Legitimate interest
GDPR Art. 6(1)(f)
Privacy notice + opt-out link
Processing category
Contractual necessity
GDPR Art. 6(1)(b)
Terms of service acceptance
Service-wide
Employee consent
Employment law / policy
Policy acknowledgement form
Workforce AI use

In practice, most enterprise-facing Claude features for end users rely on explicit opt-in. Legitimate interest is more common for internal operational use (e.g., log analysis, monitoring) where direct user interaction is not possible.
Consent Record Data Model
A consent record must capture enough context to answer an auditor's questions: who consented, to what, under what version of the notice, and for how long.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from typing import Optional
import uuid

@dataclass
class ConsentRecord:
    consent_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    user_id: str = ""                   # internal user/employee identifier
    feature_id: str = ""                # e.g. "claude-email-summarizer"
    legal_basis: str = ""               # "explicit_consent" | "legitimate_interest" | "contract"
    consent_version: str = ""           # privacy notice version user saw, e.g. "v2.3"
    granted_at: datetime = None         # UTC timestamp of acceptance
    expires_at: Optional[datetime] = None  # None = indefinite; set for time-limited grants
    scope: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # ["text_inference", "conversation_history"]
    source_ip: str = ""                 # for audit trail
    ui_element: str = ""                # "modal_v2" | "inline_checkbox" | "policy_banner"
    withdrawn_at: Optional[datetime] = None  # populated on revocation

    def is_active(self) -> bool:
        if self.withdrawn_at:
            return False
        if self.expires_at and datetime.now(timezone.utc) > self.expires_at:
            return False
        return True

The scope list is critical: it ties this consent record to specific Claude processing activities. A user who consented to text summarization has not automatically consented to having their messages used for conversation history or retrieval indexing.
Consent Store Implementation
Persisting Records

Consent records must survive restarts, be auditable, and support efficient lookups by user and feature. A relational store is the natural fit because foreign-key relationships and SQL joins make the audit queries straightforward.

import sqlite3
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from consent_model import ConsentRecord

DB_PATH = Path("/var/data/consent/consent_store.db")

def init_db(db_path: Path = DB_PATH):
    with sqlite3.connect(db_path) as conn:
        conn.execute("""
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS consent_records (
                consent_id      TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                user_id         TEXT NOT NULL,
                feature_id      TEXT NOT NULL,
                legal_basis     TEXT NOT NULL,
                consent_version TEXT NOT NULL,
                granted_at      TEXT NOT NULL,
                expires_at      TEXT,
                scope           TEXT NOT NULL,   -- JSON array
                source_ip       TEXT,
                ui_element      TEXT,
                withdrawn_at    TEXT
            )
        """)
        conn.execute("CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_user_feature ON consent_records(user_id, feature_id)")

def save_consent(record: ConsentRecord, db_path: Path = DB_PATH):
    with sqlite3.connect(db_path) as conn:
        conn.execute("""
            INSERT INTO consent_records VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)
        """, (
            record.consent_id,
            record.user_id,
            record.feature_id,
            record.legal_basis,
            record.consent_version,
            record.granted_at.isoformat(),
            record.expires_at.isoformat() if record.expires_at else None,
            json.dumps(record.scope),
            record.source_ip,
            record.ui_element,
            record.withdrawn_at.isoformat() if record.withdrawn_at else None,
        ))

def get_active_consent(user_id: str, feature_id: str, db_path: Path = DB_PATH) -> ConsentRecord | None:
    """Return the most recent active consent record, or None."""
    with sqlite3.connect(db_path) as conn:
        conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row
        row = conn.execute("""
            SELECT * FROM consent_records
            WHERE user_id = ? AND feature_id = ? AND withdrawn_at IS NULL
            ORDER BY granted_at DESC LIMIT 1
        """, (user_id, feature_id)).fetchone()
    if not row:
        return None
    record = ConsentRecord(**{k: row[k] for k in row.keys()})
    record.scope = json.loads(record.scope)
    # parse datetime strings back
    record.granted_at = datetime.fromisoformat(record.granted_at)
    if record.expires_at:
        record.expires_at = datetime.fromisoformat(record.expires_at)
    return record if record.is_active() else None
Consent Withdrawal

def withdraw_consent(user_id: str, feature_id: str, db_path: Path = DB_PATH) -> int:
    """Mark all active consent records for this user+feature as withdrawn.
    Returns the number of records updated."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    with sqlite3.connect(db_path) as conn:
        result = conn.execute("""
            UPDATE consent_records
            SET withdrawn_at = ?
            WHERE user_id = ? AND feature_id = ? AND withdrawn_at IS NULL
        """, (now, user_id, feature_id))
        return result.rowcount
Gating Claude API Calls on Consent

The consent check must happen in the application layer before constructing the Anthropic API request. The token identifying the consent record should travel with the request as a system-prompt annotation so it can be recorded in the audit log alongside the conversation.

import anthropic
from consent_store import get_active_consent, DB_PATH
from consent_model import ConsentRecord

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class ConsentRequiredError(Exception):
    pass

def call_claude_with_consent(
    user_id: str,
    feature_id: str,
    user_message: str,
    required_scope: list[str],
) -> str:
    """
    Invoke Claude only when a valid, scoped consent record exists.
    Raises ConsentRequiredError if consent is absent or insufficient.
    """
    record: ConsentRecord | None = get_active_consent(user_id, feature_id)
    if record is None:
        raise ConsentRequiredError(
            f"No active consent for user={user_id}, feature={feature_id}"
        )

    # Verify scope covers the requested activities
    missing = set(required_scope) - set(record.scope)
    if missing:
        raise ConsentRequiredError(
            f"Consent scope {record.scope} does not cover {missing}"
        )

    system = (
        f"[consent_id={record.consent_id} "
        f"user={user_id} feature={feature_id} "
        f"legal_basis={record.legal_basis}]\n"
        "You are a helpful enterprise assistant. Process only the information "
        "the user explicitly provides in this session."
    )

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

Embedding the consent_id in the system prompt means every token of Claude's response is traceable to a specific consent record in your audit log — a property that satisfies most regulatory audit requirements without any post-hoc reconstruction.
Consent Dialog Design Requirements

The UI element that captures consent must satisfy legal validity requirements to make the resulting record defensible. A pre-checked checkbox, a buried clause in terms of service, or a dialog that makes it harder to decline than to accept all undermine the legal basis.
Use granular checkboxes rather than a single 'I agree to everything' toggle — one checkbox per processing scope (inference, history, retrieval indexing).
Present the privacy notice version number and a link to the full text in the dialog so the consent_version field in the record is meaningful.
Make the 'Decline' button equally prominent as the 'Accept' button — GDPR Recital 32 requires consent to be freely given.
Log both the accept and the decline events, including the UI element identifier, so you can demonstrate the dialog was shown even when users refused.
Provide an in-product consent management page so users can review and withdraw consent without contacting support.

# Example: log the consent dialog outcome regardless of accept/decline
from datetime import datetime, timezone
import json
from pathlib import Path

DIALOG_LOG = Path("/var/data/consent/dialog_events.jsonl")

def log_dialog_event(
    user_id: str,
    feature_id: str,
    outcome: str,           # "accepted" | "declined" | "dismissed"
    ui_element: str,
    consent_version: str,
    source_ip: str = "",
):
    event = {
        "ts": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "user_id": user_id,
        "feature_id": feature_id,
        "outcome": outcome,
        "ui_element": ui_element,
        "consent_version": consent_version,
        "source_ip": source_ip,
    }
    with open(DIALOG_LOG, "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(event) + "\n")
Consent Versioning and Re-Capture

Privacy notices change. When the enterprise updates the AI data processing notice — to add a new Claude feature, change a retention period, or reflect a model upgrade — existing consent records tied to the old version may no longer be sufficient. The consent management system must track notice versions and trigger re-capture when the gap is material.

Integrate this check into the consent gate: if the stored record passes the active/scope checks but consent_needs_refresh returns True, treat it the same as a missing record and surface the consent dialog with the updated notice.
Summary

Consent capture for Claude-powered features requires four components working together: a data model that records who consented to what under which legal basis and notice version, a persistent store that survives restarts and supports tamper-evident audit queries, an API gate that checks and attaches consent context before every Claude call, and a UI layer that collects consent in a legally valid manner. Consent versioning ensures that material changes to data processing practices trigger re-capture rather than silently relying on stale records.
